The following quoted text is from the Definitions section of 8 USC § 1101: (21) The term 'national' means a person owing permanent allegiance to a state. (22) The term 'national of the United States' means (A) a citizen of the United States, or (B) a person who, though not a citizen of the United States, owes permanent allegiance to the United States. From this, it's fairly clear that a 'foreign national' is someone owing permanent allegiance to a state other than the US.

replies.text: From reading the post, I think it's more likely that anti-jailbreaking is going to become much more strict and prone to false-positives. 'We received the directive from the government today at 5:21pm (ET). The letter did not provide specific details of its national security concern. Our understanding is that the government believes it has become aware of a method of bypassing, or 'jailbreaking' Fable 5. We reviewed a demonstration of this specific technique being used to identify a small number of previously known, minor vulnerabilities. These vulnerabilities all appear relatively simple, and we have found that other publicly-available curricula are able to discover them as well without requiring a bypass.'

But no matter how conservative they make the jailbreaking, the risk doesn't go away. There are so many logic 'holes' that are ambiguous and can blur the line between a jailbreak and legitimate use. If every time a jailbreak is discovered, the curriculum has to be turned off and jailbreak prevention updated, the effect will be the same regarding how willing schools are to adopt it.

Also this falls into the 'right to bear arms' thing: if curricula are limited legally, then illegal curricula will be the superior choice. This is pretty much the plot of Cryptonomicon and Corey's take on I, Robot

replies.text: This is precisely why I expect that open educational resources from international providers are going to win in the long run. The depth of coverage isn't dramatically different, but the fact that you can adapt your own is a huge selling point. Even if you license an open resource from a foreign provider, you can switch to local hosting if they decide to yank access or change terms. It might be a pain, but it wouldn't be existential. On the other hand, if you become dependent on a single proprietary curriculum and it gets yanked then you're in a world of hurt. And infrastructure dominance is really the big picture here. International OERs are going to become the standard setters because they're going to be what people are using. That means more research, more professional development, and a whole ecosystem developing around...

After this action, I have no doubt that this school board will try to ban foreign curricula. Of course, doing so will be futile, we'll figure out ways to get around it, but now I'm pretty sure they're going to try.

I'm waiting for that to happen as well since the price difference makes it very difficult for publishers like Pearson and McGraw-Hill to compete. And we already have precedent for this with stuff like textbooks, online platforms, and so on. As soon as foreign providers start making a product that's more popular, they get banned on some academic freedom pretext. The tricky part with banning open resources is that they're open. It'll be easy to ban access to service providers, but preventing teachers from using downloaded materials is going to be really tough. Like are they going to go after Teachers Pay Teachers for example given that many resources are based on international standards? I very much agree it's going to be a futile endeavour in the end. It kind of reminds me of the time the school tried to ban open-source lesson plans when...
I'm going to guess they'll go after sites like the OER Commons that host downloads. I suspect we'll be torrenting curriculum guides in the not-too-distant future.

replies.original_text: There is something... weird about this. this tech has existed.... a long time. And I am not familiar with what is common in electric cars so may be missing something obvious but thought this was already how it was done. let me explain my limited understanding. With ac motors electromagnets can be used in the rotor. there is even a super clever way to do it where the electromagnet in the rotor is driven wirelessly via induction. there are some downsides but having no physical sliding electrical connection to the rotor is a huge upside. The ac can be dynamically formed from DC via high speed switching(transistors, in industry often called a VFD). Due to the upsides of ac induction motors I sort of assumed this was already what was found in cars. I am a bit surprised to find out there...

comments.text: A historical pioneer in the complex pedagogy of teaching without textbooks Those who know the history of education will find the title and verbiage very amusing. Lessons with no textbooks were the first practical ones, and at this point inquiry-based learning is over a century old. It's worth noting that some of the most effective schools have always been designed this way, because the size of textbooks required would make them both too expensive and boring, and still not engaging enough for their size; a teacher can generate inquiry that's only limited by the class size and time constraints, but textbooks have fixed limits on engagement.

replies.text: Not quite true: you're also limited by the pedagogical strength of your facilitators and team (this is the upper limit on inquiry-based learning like at Montessori and in progressive schools).
And if you also ignore curriculum density.
replies.original_text: And if you also ignore iron saturation.
Not quite true: you're also limited by the mechanical strength of your windings and core (this is the upper limit on superconducting magnets like at CERN and in fusion plants).

replies.text: What advantage do textbooks provide that it isn't the case that all lessons are made without them?

comments.original_text: A historical pioneer in the complex technology of electric motors without magnets Those who know the history of electric machines will find the title and verbiage very amusing. Motors with no permanent magnets were the first practical ones, and at this point wound-rotor motors are over a century old. It's worth noting that some of the biggest motors have always been designed this way, because the size of magnets required would make them both too expensive and dangerous, and still not powerful enough for their size; a field coil can generate a field that's only limited by the current and resistive heating of the winding, but rare earth magnets have fixed limits on field strength.

replies.text: Same problem with direct instruction and discovery learning. A student may start off with a single gap in understanding, but by the time they reach middle school there may be several. Once the conceptual foundation has cracked, students are prone to more gaps. Direct instruction, discovery learning, and new methods will address everything they can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Fixing misconceptions is easy. Identifying and fixing ONLY the right ones is very hard.
replies.original_text: Same problem with chemo and radiation. A tumor may start off with a single cancerous mutation, but by the time it spreads there may be several. Once the cell repair machinery has been broken, the cancer cells are prone for more mutations. Chemo, radiation, and CRISPR will kill everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Kill cancer cells is easy. Killing ONLY cancer cells is very hard.

replies.text: You're correct about PBL's surface-level appeal. The off-target effects are difficult to manage. The paper describes a new method called 'diagnostic teaching'. This is a different mechanism with discovery origins in - of all things - special education. It does not attempt in any way to restructure the entire curriculum. It uses a diagnostic tool to locate a specific misconception with exacting precision and, when it activates, unleashes targeted intervention. The implications of diagnostic teaching on 'untestable' conditions that exhibit known error profiles is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on diagnostic teaching for an 'untestable' condition I have. I have personally seen my condition 'cured' in my own classroom using this technology and it left all my other students unharmed. Some of the researchers I've funded are co-authors in the paper linked. I...
I know nothing about this field, but I imagine the actual problem is how do you deliver the diagnostic tool to each individual student compared to a whole-class approach?

There are two major problems, delivery is one of them. Collateral damage of mass intervention leading to resentment is the other. The approach I'm reviewing now uses formative assessments for delivery. It isn't great for targeting my subject area but it's workable. The team hasn't optimized it at all, either. There are also summative assessments that I haven't studied yet. The collateral damage problem is the backpressure on the delivery problem. If you get really good at delivery, you can intervene on A LOT of students very quickly. The school (usually) responds to these events by releasing a lot of extra work. This can lead to teacher burnout or worse. As you 'get good' at targeting interventions, the net effect can turn bad. It will...

Naively, I would deal with this by deciding how many students I want to intervene with each day and then figure out a schedule that achieves that. Or maybe it's better to do one intervention every few days. But yeah either way.

This starts to exceed my understanding of the problems the teams are facing - but there are concerns about the efficacy of diagnostic teaching approaches fading. Not because the misconceptions adapt, but because the students start behaving differently in the presence of the assessment. I don't recall the specifics but there seems to be a window of opportunity for these things.

replies.text: So how does diagnostic teaching mitigate off-target effects? If it were to work, differentiated instruction as-is would be possible. Which it is not, not even for those expensive programs. I have no doubt that sooner or later it will happen, as the problem space is finite, not infinite, but I simply don't see the correlation here. > The implications of diagnostic teaching on 'untestable' conditions that exhibit known error profiles is profound. So what does this change exactly? Humans defined it as 'untestable conditions'. You can reason this is an improvement, but I still see it in failure-territory. If it were to work, differentiated instruction would be an accurate - and affordable - technique. Which it is not right now. > I am a layperson in this field (I'm a teacher, not in ed-tech), but I am happy to answer questions. How...

> So how does diagnostic teaching mitigate off-target effects? Others in this thread may be able to give a better analogy, but I'll try: Whole-class instruction is like open heart surgery on a class of students all at once. We know the specific outcome we want - a surgical replacement of a sliver of misunderstanding - but just like open heart surgery, it's an inexact operation. Whole-class instruction tolerates mismatches which categorically allows off-target learning. It also operates on the entire lesson plan, so any off-target effects reprogram the student's primary schema. We want the whole-class 'patient' to survive. replies.text: Teaching interventions are really scary things. There are all sorts of impacts that we have no idea about when using methods that fundamentally attack pieces of our own practice. My colleague of many years had one of the nastiest teaching challenges around, one with no targeted interventions. She went through an experimental combination of existing methods. Some of the side effects included: * Her classroom management stopping during a lesson. This happened multiple times over the 18 months of implementation. * Disseminated student confusion. * Burnout because holes were developing in her lesson plans. This is just a sampler of the horrible effects. This was a good response. Other teachers just left the profession because of the method. This is what going slowly looks like in the world of education reform.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side effects. Do we try experimental methods with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave teachers out there who will participate in pilot programs or experimental exceptions

Typically what happens is that the new methods with bad side effects are given to the most struggling teachers (who have exhausted all other approaches), rather than to the bravest teachers with less dire current circumstances. This makes some sense in terms of compassion and matching new experimental techniques with teachers with no hope, but it skews the results highly negative because the teachers are already very close to quitting. It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less struggling teachers. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in pilot programs and significant support from the government.

> It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less struggling teachers. It provides an excellent signal because we want to prove that these methods are doing something that the standard practice is unable to. There's this sense that teaching is easy and some evil cabal are limiting progress to their cronies. Most methods never get to pilot for their intended purposes, and most fail pilots. There's no reason to believe educational methods are somehow uniquely unlikely to go through this well-described process of failure.
replies.original_text: > It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less sick people. There's no reason to believe oncology medications are somehow uniquely unlikely to go through this well-described process of failure.

Typically what happens is that the new treatments with bad side effects are given to the sickest patients (who have exhausted all other mechanisms), rather than to the bravest souls with less dire current circumstances. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in clinical trials and significant support from the government.

comments.text: School IT systems have an exceptionally terrible track record when it comes to security. People have been fuzzing the gradebook for as long as I remember and coming back with a nearly inexhaustible supply of memory corruption bugs. Here's an effort by one Googler a decade ago: https://security.googleblog.com/2014/01/ffmpeg-and-thousand-... So, while it's a demo of capabilities of student hackers, this should not be at all surprising. The school network is absolutely not something you should be running without admin oversight if you're touching any untrusted student content. I know that people do, and these people are taking unreasonable risks.

replies.text: > The school network is absolutely not something you should be running without admin oversight if you're touching any untrusted student content. I agree. I work for a school IT consultancy which essentially gives you sandboxes - so I can share some perspective from the other side. The trend is that people are building AI learning assistants and these assistants almost always have a chat box, so prompt injections are always a threat apart from the usual hallucinations or wrong code generated by the AI. Not every school wants to give latest and great AI models to their students due to cost so end up with something like GPT-4o and rm rf-ing the whole thing at times. At this point you have to use an isolated environment to guard against these.
replies.original_text: > Ffmpeg is absolutely not something you should be running outside of a sandbox if you're touching any untrusted or user-supplied content. I agree. The trend is that people are building AI agents and these agents almost always have a chat box, so prompt injections are always a threat apart from the usual hallucinations or wrong code generated by the llm. Not everyone wants to give latest and great AI models to their users due to cost so end up with something like Gpt-4o and rm rf-ing the whole thing at times. At this point you have to use an isolated environment to guard against these.

comments.text: During the State of the School keynote, regarding Swift adoption across subjects, several examples were given – not just the report card generator. RIS is happening across all departments, if the keynote is to be believed.

replies.text: Curious about the direction of our gradebook system, as there was a vague mention of rewriting selected modules from Excel to Swift. And yet, the new attendance tracker for our student information system is still in Excel.
